He also identifies recruitment and apartheid-era social constraints as part of why a larger naval commitment was politically difficult.

evidence.note: Cape geography, force requirements, and domestic constraints are examined.

title: Why French parity with Italy was strategically awkward
videoTitle: Mistakes & Missed Opportunities of the Washington Naval Treaties
summary: France's equal treaty position with Italy did not match France's wider imperial commitments or its direct Mediterranean rivalry.
body: Clarke's criticism is not focused on one French hull, but on the strategic consequences of accepting the same naval scale as Italy. France faced both Mediterranean competition and broad imperial commitments, while Italy's principal concerns were more concentrated. The comparison illustrates his wider point that equal treaty ratios could conceal unequal strategic burdens.

evidence.note: French-Italian parity is contrasted with their different strategic situations.

title: Cruisers as presence ships and industrial investment
videoTitle: Mistakes & Missed Opportunities of the Washington Naval Treaties
summary: With cruiser tonnage initially unrestricted, regular construction could have supported imperial presence, shipyards, design expertise, and employment.
body: The lecture stresses that Washington allowed as many cruisers as governments were prepared to fund beneath the displacement ceiling. Clarke argues that regular orders would have created both operational presence and a durable domestic industrial base. He frames naval spending as an economic choice with effects in shipyard employment, technical capacity, and regional development.

evidence.note: Unrestricted cruiser construction and its industrial effects are discussed.

title: Indian sloops and vulnerable coastal logistics
videoTitle: Mistakes & Missed Opportunities of the Washington Naval Treaties
summary: More Indian-built sloops could have supported local industry while protecting a coastal transport system exposed during wartime.
body: Clarke connects the Indian Navy's limited sloop force to the movement of food and goods by sea around the subcontinent. Once wartime threats disrupted that traffic, there were too few escorts and too few replacements available from a globally stretched British system. He also sees larger Indian naval construction as a way to develop industrial capability and visible national capacity.

evidence.note: India's cruiser and sloop possibilities are tied to domestic sea transport and wartime escort needs.

title: Kaga conversion and Japan's treaty flexibility
videoTitle: Mistakes & Missed Opportunities of the Washington Naval Treaties
summary: The lecture questions Kaga's conversion after Amagi's loss and explores how Japan could have sought a more efficient carrier or hidden submarine growth.
body: Clarke argues that a slower converted Kaga was a poor substitute for the fast carrier he believes naval aviation required, and suggests Japan could have asked to build a new carrier instead. He then turns to Japan's geography and control of access to its yards as advantages for concealing submarine construction. Both examples frame treaty limits as something states could exploit more imaginatively, or evade.

evidence.note: Amagi, Kaga, carrier speed, and Japanese submarine concealment are discussed.

title: A Dominion sloop program as wartime mobilization insurance
videoTitle: Mistakes & Missed Opportunities of the Washington Naval Treaties
summary: Regular sloop orders across the Empire could have preserved escorts, trained workers, and shipyard capacity before the next war.
body: Using a hypothetical annual construction program, Clarke shows how modest orders could accumulate into a large reserve of useful ships by 1939. The larger payoff is industrial: yards, turbine production, and skilled labor would already be prepared to expand into destroyers or other wartime requirements. The point is less the exact total than the value of a sustained building rhythm.

evidence.note: The hypothetical Empire-wide sloop program and its mobilization effects are explained.

title: US cruiser shortages and the culture of scarce assets
videoTitle: Mistakes & Missed Opportunities of the Washington Naval Treaties
summary: A larger prewar cruiser force could have improved US naval presence, design development, and officers' experience of calculated risk.
body: Clarke describes cruisers as the ships that make global presence possible when capital-ship construction is constrained. He also argues that scarcity can train commanders to protect valuable ships too carefully, while large destroyer forces more readily teach officers to judge necessary operational risk. The claim is institutional rather than a criticism of individual wartime commanders.

evidence.note: US cruiser construction, presence missions, and the effect of scarce assets on command culture are discussed.

title: The missed Lexington battlecruiser option
videoTitle: Mistakes & Missed Opportunities of the Washington Naval Treaties
summary: Keeping two Lexingtons as battlecruisers could have given American carriers fast capital-ship escorts and a stronger scouting force.
body: Clarke does not call the Colorado choice wrong; he instead contrasts a stronger battle line with the speed and reach the United States lacked in its scouting force. Modernized Lexington battlecruisers could have operated alongside carriers, supported risky strikes, and delayed stronger enemy ships long enough for a force to escape. The discussion uses Force H and Renown as an example of how fast capital ships can enable carrier operations.

evidence.note: The Colorado-Lexington trade-off and battlecruisers as carrier escorts are examined.

title: Britain's missed escort and fast-capital-ship options
videoTitle: Mistakes & Missed Opportunities of the Washington Naval Treaties
summary: Britain's underfunded sloop program and the abandoned F3 design illustrate how repeated small savings could reduce future flexibility.
body: Clarke criticizes interrupted Australian and British small-warship construction because lost yard capacity and escorts proved costly once war arrived. He then describes the F3 as a missed alternative to Nelson and Rodney: a fast ship with 15-inch/50-calibre guns could have influenced later modernization and German design calculations. The argument is about a different capability mix, not a claim that the Nelson class was a bad choice.

evidence.note: Australian and British sloop procurement leads into the F3 fast-battleship counterfactual.
